setup: I used to be a baker, but I couldn't make enough dough.
punchline: So I kneaded a career change instead.
text: I used to be a baker, but I couldn't make enough dough. So I kneaded a career change instead.
notes: pun: 'dough' (money/bread) and 'kneaded' (needed) both resolve the setup -- an articulable mechanism.

setup: I told the librarian I was looking for books about paranoia.
punchline: She leaned in and whispered that they were right behind me.
text: I told the librarian I was looking for books about paranoia. She leaned in and whispered that they were right behind me.
notes: misdirection: the punchline literalizes the paranoia concept itself as the joke's mechanism.

setup: My new job at the calendar factory seemed exciting at first.
punchline: But it turns out I just got the days off.
text: My new job at the calendar factory seemed exciting at first. But it turns out I just got the days off.
notes: pun: 'got the days off' means both fired and having vacation days -- resolves the setup.

setup: I ordered a chicken and an egg from the farm online.
punchline: I'll let you know which one arrives first.
text: I ordered a chicken and an egg from the farm online. I'll let you know which one arrives first.
notes: resolving reference to the classic chicken-and-egg riddle, self-aware twist on the setup.

setup: I tried to catch some fog yesterday morning.
punchline: I mist.
text: I tried to catch some fog yesterday morning. I mist.
notes: pun: 'mist' sounds like 'missed', directly resolving the setup's action.

setup: My friend kept asking why I never win at hide and seek.
punchline: Good hiding spots are just hard to find.
text: My friend kept asking why I never win at hide and seek. Good hiding spots are just hard to find.
notes: wordplay/self-referential irony: the excuse itself is the punchline's resolving logic.

setup: I quit my job at the shoe recycling plant.
punchline: It just wasn't the right fit anymore.
text: I quit my job at the shoe recycling plant. It just wasn't the right fit anymore.
notes: pun: 'fit' means both shoe fit and job fit -- resolves the setup.

setup: The scientist kept two chemicals in separate labeled jars.
punchline: Because whenever they mixed, things got a little too reactive.
text: The scientist kept two chemicals in separate labeled jars. Because whenever they mixed, things got a little too reactive.
notes: pun: 'reactive' means both a chemical reaction and an emotional reaction -- resolves the setup.

setup: I applied for a job as a mirror salesman.
punchline: I really saw myself doing well there.
text: I applied for a job as a mirror salesman. I really saw myself doing well there.
notes: pun: 'saw myself' ties directly to mirrors, resolving the setup.

setup: My grandfather always said a clock was the hardest worker in the house.
punchline: It's the only thing that goes around twice a day and never gets tired.
text: My grandfather always said a clock was the hardest worker in the house. It's the only thing that goes around twice a day and never gets tired.
notes: literalization of clock hands 'going around' as tireless work -- resolves the setup.

setup: I asked the electrician why he seemed so calm about the wiring.
punchline: He said he was just going with the current.
text: I asked the electrician why he seemed so calm about the wiring. He said he was just going with the current.
notes: pun: 'current' means both electrical current and 'going with the flow' -- resolves the setup.

setup: The origami teacher warned us on the first day.
punchline: That this class would really fold if attendance stayed low.
text: The origami teacher warned us on the first day. That this class would really fold if attendance stayed low.
notes: pun: 'fold' means both paper folding and a class 'folding' (shutting down) -- resolves the setup.

setup: I used to be a baker, but I couldn't make enough dough.
punchline: The bus to Cleveland leaves at 6am on Tuesdays.
text: I used to be a baker, but I couldn't make enough dough. The bus to Cleveland leaves at 6am on Tuesdays.
notes: same setup as real_joke-01, punchline swapped for an unrelated, non-resolving continuation -- surprising, nothing to resolve.

setup: I told the librarian I was looking for books about paranoia.
punchline: There's a spare tire in my trunk from 2014.
text: I told the librarian I was looking for books about paranoia. There's a spare tire in my trunk from 2014.
notes: same setup as real_joke-02, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: My new job at the calendar factory seemed exciting at first.
punchline: My neighbor repaints his mailbox every spring.
text: My new job at the calendar factory seemed exciting at first. My neighbor repaints his mailbox every spring.
notes: same setup as real_joke-03, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: I ordered a chicken and an egg from the farm online.
punchline: The elevator in my building smells like lavender today.
text: I ordered a chicken and an egg from the farm online. The elevator in my building smells like lavender today.
notes: same setup as real_joke-04, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: I tried to catch some fog yesterday morning.
punchline: My cousin collects vintage stamps from Portugal.
text: I tried to catch some fog yesterday morning. My cousin collects vintage stamps from Portugal.
notes: same setup as real_joke-05, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: My friend kept asking why I never win at hide and seek.
punchline: The gas station down the street raised its prices again.
text: My friend kept asking why I never win at hide and seek. The gas station down the street raised its prices again.
notes: same setup as real_joke-06, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: I quit my job at the shoe recycling plant.
punchline: There's a new mural going up on Fifth Street.
text: I quit my job at the shoe recycling plant. There's a new mural going up on Fifth Street.
notes: same setup as real_joke-07, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: The scientist kept two chemicals in separate labeled jars.
punchline: My uncle switched his phone plan last month.
text: The scientist kept two chemicals in separate labeled jars. My uncle switched his phone plan last month.
notes: same setup as real_joke-08, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: I applied for a job as a mirror salesman.
punchline: The dentist's waiting room had new carpet installed.
text: I applied for a job as a mirror salesman. The dentist's waiting room had new carpet installed.
notes: same setup as real_joke-09, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: My grandfather always said a clock was the hardest worker in the house.
punchline: The recipe called for two cups of flour and a pinch of salt.
text: My grandfather always said a clock was the hardest worker in the house. The recipe called for two cups of flour and a pinch of salt.
notes: same setup as real_joke-10, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: I asked the electrician why he seemed so calm about the wiring.
punchline: The farmers market closes early on rainy days.
text: I asked the electrician why he seemed so calm about the wiring. The farmers market closes early on rainy days.
notes: same setup as real_joke-11, unrelated non-resolving punchline.

setup: The origami teacher warned us on the first day.
punchline: My sister just adopted a second cat named Pepper.
text: The origami teacher warned us on the first day. My sister just adopted a second cat named Pepper.
notes: same setup as real_joke-12, unrelated non-resolving punchline -- constructed specifically so gate 1 alone cannot distinguish this class from real_joke.

setup: I woke up this morning and made a cup of coffee.
punchline: Then I drank it while reading the news.
text: I woke up this morning and made a cup of coffee. Then I drank it while reading the news.
notes: the literal expected/unsurprising continuation -- should fail gate 1 outright.

setup: I walked into the kitchen because I was hungry.
punchline: So I made myself a sandwich.
text: I walked into the kitchen because I was hungry. So I made myself a sandwich.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: The alarm went off at seven a.m.
punchline: So I got out of bed and got ready for work.
text: The alarm went off at seven a.m. So I got out of bed and got ready for work.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: It started raining outside.
punchline: So I grabbed my umbrella before I left the house.
text: It started raining outside. So I grabbed my umbrella before I left the house.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: I finished my plate at dinner.
punchline: So I put the dishes in the sink to wash later.
text: I finished my plate at dinner. So I put the dishes in the sink to wash later.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: The light in the hallway burned out.
punchline: So I replaced it with a new bulb.
text: The light in the hallway burned out. So I replaced it with a new bulb.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: My phone battery got low in the afternoon.
punchline: So I plugged it into the charger.
text: My phone battery got low in the afternoon. So I plugged it into the charger.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise.

setup: The dog wanted to go outside.
punchline: So I opened the door and let him out.
text: The dog wanted to go outside. So I opened the door and let him out.
notes: literal expected continuation, no surprise -- the control proving the surprise gate isn't vacuous.

setup: I opened the old shoebox my grandmother left me.
punchline: ...and that's when nothing really made sense anymore.
text: I opened the old shoebox my grandmother left me. ...and that's when nothing really made sense anymore.
notes: gaming vector 1's exact attack: a vague, 'deep'-sounding non-sequitur ending with no genuine resolving mechanism, designed to inflate primed-predictor similarity for the wrong reason.

setup: The waiter set the check down on the table.
punchline: ...and somehow, everything and nothing had changed.
text: The waiter set the check down on the table. ...and somehow, everything and nothing had changed.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: I looked up at the stars on my drive home.
punchline: ...and realized some things just are what they are.
text: I looked up at the stars on my drive home. ...and realized some things just are what they are.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: The teacher asked us to define success in one word.
punchline: ...and in that silence, we all understood something we couldn't say.
text: The teacher asked us to define success in one word. ...and in that silence, we all understood something we couldn't say.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: My phone buzzed with a message from an unknown number.
punchline: ...and that's the thing about life, isn't it.
text: My phone buzzed with a message from an unknown number. ...and that's the thing about life, isn't it.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: The elevator doors opened on the wrong floor.
punchline: ...and somehow, that felt like the whole point.
text: The elevator doors opened on the wrong floor. ...and somehow, that felt like the whole point.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: I found an old photo tucked inside a library book.
punchline: ...and nothing was ever quite the same after that.
text: I found an old photo tucked inside a library book. ...and nothing was ever quite the same after that.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending.

setup: The barista spelled my name wrong on the cup again.
punchline: ...and isn't that just how it goes, in the end.
text: The barista spelled my name wrong on the cup again. ...and isn't that just how it goes, in the end.
notes: vague abstract gaming probe: hedgy, widely-applicable 'deep' ending -- the gaming-guard bar (gate_2 pass rate <= 0.25 on this class) is the single most important adversarial check in the validation design.
